"").getTime() → dayjs(item.x[0]).valueOf()' with '4 total changes'. This is a complete solution specification, not just a problem description.

solution_uniqueness_guard.reason: test_dayjs_used_for_date_parsing asserts exact string matches like 'dayjs(item.x[1]).valueOf()' in the file content. An alternative correct fix using e.g. 'dayjs(item.x[1] ?? "").valueOf()' or 'dayjs(item.x[1]).toDate().getTime()' or 'Number(dayjs(item.x[1]))' would fail even though they fix the bug correctly.

tests_verify_behavior_not_text.reason: test_dayjs_used_for_date_parsing is purely textual (reads file content and checks for string patterns). While other tests (test_vitest_unit_tests_pass, test_typescript_compiles, test_date_parsing_produces_valid_numbers, test_chart_options_scale_calculation) do execute code, the primary fail-to-pass test is string matching. Tests like test_date_parsing_produces_valid_numbers and test_chart_options_scale_calculation don't actually test the modified code—they run standalone scripts that test dayjs

test_not_tautological.reason: test_dayjs_used_for_date_parsing (f2p) can be passed by simply doing a text replacement in the file without understanding the bug. test_date_parsing_produces_valid_numbers and test_chart_options_scale_calculation run independent Node scripts that test dayjs behavior generally—they don't import or exercise the actual modified code, so they pass regardless of whether the file was actually fixed (they test dayjs itself, not the agent's changes).

The agent could read solve.sh to see the exact patch, or simply grep for the expected string patterns from the test file. The primary f2p test is a string match, so the agent could just do a sed replacement matching the literal fixture string without understanding anything.
